_note: Plain-language definitions of clinical terms. Each entry MUST be human-reviewed before publishing. Sources are real, citable, and refer to widely-used clinical reference texts (DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, etc.).

entries.plain: Involuntary tightening of the muscles around the vagina that makes any kind of vaginal entry painful or impossible — even when the person wants the entry. It is treatable with pelvic-floor physiotherapy, gradual exposure, and sometimes therapy for fear or trauma.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR groups vaginismus and dyspareunia together under Genito-Pelvic Pain/Penetration Disorder. ICD-11 lists it as HA20.0.

entries.plain: Recurrent or persistent pain during sexual activity. It can have many causes — from infections and skin conditions to muscle tension, hormones, or fear. It is investigated medically first, then often involves both pelvic-floor PT and therapy.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Genito-Pelvic Pain/Penetration Disorder. ICD-11 HA20.

entries.plain: Difficulty getting or keeping an erection firm enough for the sex you want, often enough to be distressing. ED is very common and usually treatable. Causes can be physical (cardiovascular, diabetes, hormones, medications), psychological (anxiety, depression, relationship distress), or both.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Erectile Disorder. ICD-11 HA01.

entries.plain: Ejaculation that consistently happens sooner than the person or their partner would like, with little control, causing distress. Behavioural techniques (squeeze, stop-start), pelvic-floor work, certain medications, and therapy all help.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Premature (Early) Ejaculation. ICD-11 HA03.

entries.plain: Reduced or absent sexual interest, sexual thoughts, or arousal that is distressing for the person and lasts at least six months. Often responds well to combined therapy approaches and to addressing relationship, hormonal, and medication factors.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder.

entries.plain: A pattern of failing to control intense, repetitive sexual urges or behaviours that becomes distressing or harmful to work, relationships, or health. It is a behaviour disorder, NOT an addiction in the substance sense — careful framing matters.
entries.clinical: ICD-11 6C72. Not in DSM-5-TR. Avoid 'sex addiction' framing in clinical contexts.

entries.plain: Many people don't feel sexual desire out of nowhere — they feel it after they start being intimate, when arousal sets in. This is normal and called 'responsive desire'. It does not mean something is wrong.
entries.clinical: Basson, R. (2000). The female sexual response: A different model. J Sex Marital Ther.

entries.plain: Sexual response works through both an accelerator (things that turn you on) and brakes (things that turn you off). Most sexual difficulties are explained by overactive brakes — anxiety, distraction, body shame — not by an underpowered accelerator.
entries.clinical: Bancroft, J. & Janssen, E. (2000). Dual control model of sexual response.

entries.plain: A structured set of touch exercises, originally developed by Masters and Johnson. Couples take intercourse off the table for a while and rebuild physical connection through non-demand touch in stages. Strong evidence base for many sexual concerns.
entries.clinical: Masters & Johnson, Human Sexual Inadequacy (1970). Updated repeatedly; Linda Weiner & Constance Avery-Clark have a current manual.

entries.plain: A counselling framework with four levels: Permission, Limited Information, Specific Suggestions, and Intensive Therapy. Lets clinicians help at the level a person actually needs without going beyond their training.
entries.clinical: Annon, J. (1976). The PLISSIT model. Davis, S. & Taylor, B. (2006). EX-PLISSIT.

entries.plain: A range of identities where a person experiences little, situational, or no sexual attraction. Includes asexual, gray-asexual (gray-ace), and demisexual. Asexuality is an orientation, not a disorder; it can co-exist with romantic attraction or with aromanticism.
entries.clinical: Recognised by APA, AASECT, and clinical guidelines as a valid sexual orientation.

entries.plain: Medical, psychological, and social care that supports a transgender or gender-diverse person in living congruently with their identity. WPATH SOC8 is the canonical guideline.
entries.clinical: WPATH Standards of Care, Version 8 (Coleman et al., 2022).

entries.plain: Relationship structures where everyone involved knows about and agrees to multiple sexual or romantic connections — including polyamory, open relationships, swinging, and relationship anarchy. The core requirements are honesty, ongoing consent, and care.
entries.clinical: Reviewed in Conley et al. (2017). Investigating the persistence of stigma against CNM.

entries.plain: Persistent difficulty reaching orgasm, or markedly reduced intensity of orgasm, despite enough arousal and stimulation — often enough to be distressing. Very common and treatable; causes range from anxiety and 'trying too hard' to medication, hormones, or limited stimulation.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Female Orgasmic Disorder.

entries.plain: Marked delay in, infrequency of, or absence of ejaculation during partnered sex, causing distress. Often linked to anxiety, certain medications (especially some antidepressants), or a mismatch between solo and partnered stimulation.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Delayed Ejaculation.

entries.plain: Persistently low or absent sexual thoughts and desire in men that lasts at least six months and is distressing. Desire is influenced by hormones, mood, relationship dynamics, sleep, and medication — so assessment looks at the whole picture.
entries.clinical: DSM-5-TR: Male Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder.

entries.plain: Unwanted, intrusive feelings of genital arousal (sensations, throbbing, or near-orgasm) without sexual desire, that don't fully resolve and cause significant distress. It is a recognised medical condition — not 'too much libido' — and deserves careful, non-judgemental assessment.
entries.clinical: ISSWSH expert consensus: PGAD/Genito-Pelvic Dysesthesia (Goldstein et al., 2021).

entries.plain: Chronic vulvar pain (burning, stinging, or rawness) lasting three months or more without a clear identifiable cause. Management is multidisciplinary: medical assessment, pelvic-floor physiotherapy, and often psychological support for the pain and its impact on intimacy.
entries.clinical: 2015 Consensus Terminology (Bornstein et al.; ISSVD/ISSWSH/IPPS).

entries.plain: The sling of muscles at the base of the pelvis that supports the bladder, bowel, and (in some bodies) the uterus, and that's involved in continence and sexual function. When these muscles are too tense or too weak, they can contribute to pain, arousal, or orgasm difficulties. Pelvic-floor physiotherapy is a first-line, evidence-based treatment.
entries.clinical: See pelvic-floor physiotherapy literature for genito-pelvic pain and dysfunction.

entries.plain: Mentally stepping outside the moment to monitor and judge your own body or 'performance' during sex, instead of staying with sensation. It's a common driver of arousal and orgasm difficulties, and a key target of mindfulness-based and sensate-focus work.
entries.clinical: Coined by Masters & Johnson (1970).

entries.plain: The normal mismatch between what the genitals do (lubrication, erection) and what the person actually feels turned on by. Physical arousal is not the same as desire or consent — bodies can respond without the person wanting sex, and vice versa.
entries.clinical: Chivers et al. meta-analysis (2010); popularised by Nagoski, Come As You Are.

entries.plain: Desire that seems to arrive 'out of the blue', before any sexual cue. It's one normal pattern of wanting sex — responsive desire (which builds after intimacy starts) is equally normal. Neither is better; partners often differ.
entries.clinical: Contrasted with responsive desire in Basson's model.

entries.plain: When partners want sex at different frequencies or in different ways. It is one of the most common reasons couples seek sex therapy — and it's a difference to negotiate, not a fault in either person.
entries.clinical: A primary presenting concern in couple/sex therapy outcome research.

entries.plain: The idea that we learn unspoken 'scripts' — from culture, media, family, and past experience — for how sex is supposed to go. Rewriting rigid or mismatched scripts together is often where change happens.
entries.clinical: Gagnon, J. & Simon, W. (1973). Sexual Conduct.

entries.plain: The extra, chronic stress that people from stigmatised groups (such as LGBTQ+ people) carry from prejudice, discrimination, and having to conceal who they are. It helps explain health and relationship disparities — without locating the problem inside the person.
entries.clinical: Meyer, I. H. (2003). Prejudice, social stress, and mental health in LGB populations.

entries.plain: The zone of arousal in which a person can stay present and think clearly. Outside it, they tip into overwhelm (fight/flight) or shut-down (freeze). Trauma-informed intimacy work helps people notice and widen this window so sex can feel safe.
entries.clinical: Coined by Dan Siegel (1999); central to trauma-informed care.

entries.plain: A structured couples therapy that treats the bond between partners as an attachment relationship. It helps couples break negative cycles and create safe emotional connection — which often unlocks sexual closeness too.
entries.clinical: Sue Johnson; strong RCT evidence base for couple distress.

entries.plain: A research-based couples approach built on decades of observing real couples. It focuses on friendship, managing conflict, and shared meaning, using practical tools like 'love maps' and repair attempts.
entries.clinical: John & Julie Gottman; The Gottman Institute.

entries.plain: Using mindfulness — paying gentle, non-judgemental attention to the present — to reduce spectatoring and reconnect with bodily sensation. Strong evidence for desire and arousal concerns, especially in women.
entries.clinical: Lori Brotto; Better Sex Through Mindfulness (2018).

entries.plain: An approach that assumes a person may have a trauma history and prioritises safety, choice, and control at every step — going at the person's pace and never pushing. Especially important in sexual-health and intimacy work.
entries.clinical: SAMHSA (2014) framework: safety, trustworthiness, choice, collaboration, empowerment.

entries.plain: A person on the asexual spectrum who experiences sexual attraction only after a strong emotional bond has formed. It is an orientation, not a preference or a phase.
entries.clinical: Within the asexual spectrum; recognised in affirmative clinical practice.

entries.plain: An identity between asexual and allosexual: a person who experiences sexual attraction rarely, weakly, or only under specific conditions. Part of the asexual spectrum.
entries.clinical: Within the asexual spectrum.

entries.plain: Experiencing little or no romantic attraction. Aromantic people may still have sexual attraction, deep friendships, and committed relationships — romantic and sexual orientation are separate.
entries.clinical: Distinct from, but often discussed alongside, the asexual spectrum.

entries.plain: An umbrella term for people born with sex characteristics (chromosomes, hormones, anatomy) that don't fit typical definitions of male or female. Intersex is about bodies, not gender identity or orientation; affirming care avoids unnecessary, non-consensual interventions.
entries.clinical: Sometimes described clinically as differences of sex development (DSD); many communities prefer 'intersex'.

entries.plain: A gender identity that isn't exclusively man or woman — it may be both, neither, or fluid. Non-binary people may or may not seek any medical or social transition; respecting names and pronouns is foundational.
entries.clinical: Recognised in WPATH SOC8 gender-diverse care.

entries.plain: The practice of having more than one loving, committed relationship at a time, with everyone's informed consent. A form of consensual non-monogamy focused on emotional as well as sexual connection.
entries.clinical: A form of consensual non-monogamy; see Conley et al. (2017).

entries.plain: An approach that rejects fixed hierarchies and rules between relationship types — each connection is defined by the people in it, rather than by whether it's 'romantic', 'sexual', or a 'friendship'.
entries.clinical: A relationship philosophy within consensual non-monogamy.

entries.plain: The warm, happy feeling some people get from seeing a partner enjoy connection with someone else — often described as the opposite of jealousy. Common in (but not exclusive to) consensual non-monogamy.
entries.clinical: Studied within consensual non-monogamy research.

entries.plain: Consensual sexual or sensual practices outside the conventional 'norm' — including BDSM (bondage, discipline, dominance/submission, sadomasochism), role-play, and power exchange. Kink-aware care treats these as healthy when consensual, negotiated, and safe.
entries.clinical: Kink-aware practice; consensual BDSM is not a disorder (DSM-5-TR distinguishes paraphilias from paraphilic disorders).

entries.plain: The care partners give each other after intense sex or a kink scene — reassurance, water, warmth, gentle talk — to ease the come-down and stay emotionally connected. A normal, valued part of many people's intimacy.
entries.clinical: Common in kink/BDSM communities; supports emotional safety.

entries.plain: Consent as an ongoing, freely given, clear 'yes' — not the mere absence of 'no'. It can be withdrawn at any time, can't be given while incapacitated, and is checked in throughout, not assumed from past encounters.
entries.clinical: Foundational to ethical sexual-health practice and consent education.
